ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360043, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360564, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ad795df3e5', conversation_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ac14ec3d7a')

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="Now, I need to understand the relationships and mappings between the source and target tables. Here's what I observe:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `tier`, `region`, `industry`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `ERP_Number__c`, `Customer_Tier__c`, `Region__c`, `Industry`, etc.
   - The `id` in the source (e.g., `ACC-1001`) maps to `Id` in the target.
   - `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` should be populated from a natural key. The `customer_number` in `opportunity` (e.g., `KD-1001`) seems to be the natural key for accounts.
   - Need to map `tier` to `Customer_Tier__c` (enum: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).

2. **Contact**:
   - Source: `id`, `full_name`, `email`, `account_ref`, `company_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `FirstName`, `LastName`, `Email`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`, etc.
   - `full_name` needs to be split into `FirstName` and `LastName`.
   - `account_ref` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `CON-00001`).

3. **Opportunity**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `stage`, `amount`, `customer_number`, `account_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `StageName`, `CloseDate`, `Amount`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`, etc.
   - `customer_number` (e.g., `KD-1001`) is the natural key for the account.
   - `account_name` in the source matches `account.name` (e.g., `Lindau Neuschäfer GbR`).
   - Need to map `stage` to `StageName` (enum: Prospecting, Qualification, etc.).
   - `AccountId` must reference the Salesforce-style `Account.Id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `OPP-00001`).

4. **Project__c**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `status`, `go_live`, `client_id`, `opportunity_ref`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `Project_Status__c`, `Go_Live_Date__c`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`, etc.
   - `client_id` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `opportunity_ref` references `opportunity.id` (e.g., `OPP-00021`).
   - `Legacy_Project_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `PROJ-00001`).

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='The `full_name` values are all two-part names (e.g., "Albert Schaaf"). So, splitting on the first space should work for most cases. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account

## Project__c

- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="Now, I need to understand the relationships and mappings between the source and target tables. Here's what I observe:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `tier`, `region`, `industry`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `ERP_Number__c`, `Customer_Tier__c`, `Region__c`, `Industry`, etc.
   - The `id` in the source (e.g., `ACC-1001`) maps to `Id` in the target.
   - `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` should be populated from a natural key. The `customer_number` in `opportunity` (e.g., `KD-1001`) seems to be the natural key for accounts.
   - Need to map `tier` to `Customer_Tier__c` (enum: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).

2. **Contact**:
   - Source: `id`, `full_name`, `email`, `account_ref`, `company_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `FirstName`, `LastName`, `Email`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`, etc.
   - `full_name` needs to be split into `FirstName` and `LastName`.
   - `account_ref` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `CON-00001`).

3. **Opportunity**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `stage`, `amount`, `customer_number`, `account_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `StageName`, `CloseDate`, `Amount`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`, etc.
   - `customer_number` (e.g., `KD-1001`) is the natural key for the account.
   - `account_name` in the source matches `account.name` (e.g., `Lindau Neuschäfer GbR`).
   - Need to map `stage` to `StageName` (enum: Prospecting, Qualification, etc.).
   - `AccountId` must reference the Salesforce-style `Account.Id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `OPP-00001`).

4. **Project__c**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `status`, `go_live`, `client_id`, `opportunity_ref`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `Project_Status__c`, `Go_Live_Date__c`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`, etc.
   - `client_id` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `opportunity_ref` references `opportunity.id` (e.g., `OPP-00021`).
   - `Legacy_Project_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `PROJ-00001`).

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `serial`, `warranty`, `client`, `project`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `Serial_Number__c`, `Warranty_End_Date__c`, `Account__c`, `Project__c`, `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`, etc.
   - `client` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`) or `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`).
   - `project` references `project.id` (e.g., `PROJ-00023`).
   - `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `AST-00001`).

### Relationships:
- **Account to Opportunity**: `opportunity.customer_number` (e.g., `KD-1001`) is a natural key, but it doesn't directly appear in the `account` table. However, `opportunity.account_name` matches `account.name`.
- **Account to Contact**: `contact.account_ref` references `account.id`.
- **Account to Project**: `project.client_id` references `account.id`.
- **Opportunity to Project**: `project.opportunity_ref` references `opportunity.id`.
- **Account to Asset**: `asset.client` references `account.id` or `account.name`.
- **Project to Asset**: `asset.project` references `project.id`.

### Mappings:
- **Account Tier**: `Bronze`, `Gold`, `Silver`, `Platinum` in the source map directly to the enum in the target.
- **Opportunity Stage**: `Prospecting`, `Qualification`, `Closed Lost`, `Closed Won` in the source map directly to the enum in the target.
- **Project Status**: `Completed`, `On Hold`, `In Planning`, `Cancelled` in the source map to `Active`, `Completed`, `In Planning`, `On Hold`, `Cancelled` in the target.
- **Contact Role**: Not present in the source, so we need to determine a default or mapping.
- **Contact Preferred Language**: Not present in the source, so we need to determine a default or mapping.

### Missing Data:
- **Account**: `ERP_Number__c`, `Website`, `BillingCity`, `BillingCountry`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source. We need to provide defaults or mappings.
- **Contact**: `Phone`, `Title`, `Role__c`, `Preferred_Language__c`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Opportunity**: `CloseDate`, `CurrencyIsoCode`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Project__c**: `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.

### Defaults:
- For missing `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`, we can use `NULL` or a default timestamp.
- For `IsDeleted`, we can default to `0` (false).
- For `Website`, `BillingCity`, `BillingCountry`, we can use `NULL`.
- For `Role__c` and `Preferred_Language__c`, we can use `NULL` or a default value.
- For `CloseDate`, we can use `NULL` or a default date.
- For `CurrencyIsoCode`, we can default to `EUR` or `NULL`.

### Key Mappings:
- **Account to Opportunity**: Since `opportunity.customer_number` is not in the `account` table, but `opportunity.account_name` matches `account.name`, we can join `opportunity` to `account` using `account_name = account.name`.
- **Asset to Account**: `asset.client` can be either an `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`) or an `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`). We need to handle both cases.

### Next Steps:
1. **Account Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `tier` to `Customer_Tier__c`, `region` to `Region__c`, `industry` to `Industry`. Use `NULL` for missing fields.
2. **Contact Model**: Split `full_name` into `FirstName` and `LastName`. Map `account_ref` to `AccountId` by joining to `account.id`.
3. **Opportunity Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `stage` to `StageName`, `amount` to `Amount`. Join to `account` using `account_name = account.name` to get `AccountId`.
4. **Project__c Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `status` to `Project_Status__c`, `go_live` to `Go_Live_Date__c`. Join to `account` using `client_id = account.id` to get `Account__c`. Join to `opportunity` using `opportunity_ref = opportunity.id` to get `Opportunity__c`.
5. **Installed_Asset__c Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `serial` to `Serial_Number__c`, `warranty` to `Warranty_End_Date__c`. Handle `client` to `Account__c` by joining to `account` using either `client = account.id` or `client = account.name`. Join to `project` using `project = project.id` to get `Project__c`.

### Handling `asset.client`:
The `asset.client` field can be either:
- An `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`)
- An `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`)

We need to join `asset` to `account` using a `COALESCE` or `CASE` to handle both cases.

### Handling `full_name` Splitting:
We can split `full_name` into `FirstName` and `LastName` using `SPLIT_PART` or `REGEXP_REPLACE`.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="The `opportunity` table shows that: - Each `account_name` is associated with exactly one `customer_number`. - There are some inconsistencies in `account_name` (e.g., `Lindau Neuschäfer GRb` vs `Lindau Neuschäfer GbR`). ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360043, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360564, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ad795df3e5', conversation_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ac14ec3d7a')

response: ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='Now, the `asset.client` field contains both `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`) and `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`). The current model: 1. First tries to join `asset.client = account.id`. 2. If that fails, it uses a subquery to join `asset.client = account.name`. However, the subquery is not directly joinable in the current implementation. The current implementation uses: ```sql COALESCE( acc.id, (SELECT acc2.id FROM {{ source(\'fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\', \'account\') }} acc2 WHERE acc2.name = a.client LIMIT 1) ) AS \\"Account__c\\" ``` This should work for cases where `a.client` is an `account.name`. Let me verify: - For `a.client = \'Gnatz AG\'`, the first join (`acc.id`) will fail, and the subquery will return `acc2.id` where `acc2.name = \'Gnatz AG\'`. - For `a.client = \'ACC-1005\'`, the first join (`acc.id`) will succeed, and the subquery will not be used. This should work correctly. However, the subquery may not match if there are case or whitespace differences. To handle this, we can normalize the names in the subquery:'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/Installed_Asset__c.sql", "content": "{{ config(materialized=\'table\') }}\\n\\nSELECT\\n    a.id AS \\"Id\\",\\n    a.name AS \\"Name\\",\\n    a.serial AS \\"Serial_Number__c\\",\\n    CASE\\n        WHEN a.warranty ~ \'^\\\\d{4}-\\\\d{2}-\\\\d{2}$\' THEN a.warranty\\n        ELSE NULL\\n    END AS \\"Warranty_End_Date__c\\",\\n    COALESCE(\\n        acc.id,\\n        (SELECT acc2.id FROM {{ source(\'fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\', \'account\') }} acc2 WHERE TRIM(LOWER(acc2.name)) = TRIM(LOWER(a.client)) LIMIT 1)\\n    ) AS \\"Account__c\\",\\n    p.id AS \\"Project__c\\",\\n    a.id AS \\"Legacy_Asset_ID__c\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"CreatedDate\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"LastModifiedDate\\",\\n    0 AS \\"IsDeleted\\"\\nFROM {{ source(\'fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\', \'asset\') }} a\\nLEFT JOIN {{ source(\'fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\', \'account\') }} acc\\n    ON a.client = acc.id\\nLEFT JOIN {{ source(\'fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\', \'project\') }} p\\n    ON a.project = p.id\\n"}', tool_call_id='SNSokXMLt')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=19451, output_tokens=615), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 6, 41, 754400, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 5, 38, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-9cc48a041f86ce89', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360043, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360564, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ad795df3e5', conversation_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ac14ec3d7a')

Let me verify: - For `a.client = \'Gnatz AG\'`, the first join (`acc.id`) will fail, and the subquery will return `acc2.id` where `acc2.name = \'Gnatz AG\'`. - For `a.client = \'ACC-1005\'`, the first join (`acc.id`) will succeed, and the subquery will not be used. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360043, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360564, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ad795df3e5', conversation_id='019f5171-ff4c-748a-ad11-b5ac14ec3d7a')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 50, 53, 360032, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL

- `industry` text NULL

- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text

- `LastModifiedDate` text

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="Now, I need to understand the relationships and mappings between the source and target tables. Here's what I observe:

### Key Observations:
1. **Account**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `tier`, `region`, `industry`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `ERP_Number__c`, `Customer_Tier__c`, `Region__c`, `Industry`, etc.
   - The `id` in the source (e.g., `ACC-1001`) maps to `Id` in the target.
   - `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` should be populated from a natural key. The `customer_number` in `opportunity` (e.g., `KD-1001`) seems to be the natural key for accounts.
   - Need to map `tier` to `Customer_Tier__c` (enum: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).

2. **Contact**:
   - Source: `id`, `full_name`, `email`, `account_ref`, `company_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `FirstName`, `LastName`, `Email`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`, etc.
   - `full_name` needs to be split into `FirstName` and `LastName`.
   - `account_ref` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `CON-00001`).

3. **Opportunity**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `stage`, `amount`, `customer_number`, `account_name`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `StageName`, `CloseDate`, `Amount`, `AccountId`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`, etc.
   - `customer_number` (e.g., `KD-1001`) is the natural key for the account.
   - `account_name` in the source matches `account.name` (e.g., `Lindau Neuschäfer GbR`).
   - Need to map `stage` to `StageName` (enum: Prospecting, Qualification, etc.).
   - `AccountId` must reference the Salesforce-style `Account.Id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `OPP-00001`).

4. **Project__c**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `status`, `go_live`, `client_id`, `opportunity_ref`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `Project_Status__c`, `Go_Live_Date__c`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`, etc.
   - `client_id` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1001`).
   - `opportunity_ref` references `opportunity.id` (e.g., `OPP-00021`).
   - `Legacy_Project_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `PROJ-00001`).

5. **Installed_Asset__c**:
   - Source: `id`, `name`, `serial`, `warranty`, `client`, `project`
   - Target: `Id`, `Name`, `Serial_Number__c`, `Warranty_End_Date__c`, `Account__c`, `Project__c`, `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`, etc.
   - `client` references `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`) or `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`).
   - `project` references `project.id` (e.g., `PROJ-00023`).
   - `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` should be the source `id` (e.g., `AST-00001`).

### Relationships:
- **Account to Opportunity**: `opportunity.customer_number` (e.g., `KD-1001`) is a natural key, but it doesn't directly appear in the `account` table. However, `opportunity.account_name` matches `account.name`.
- **Account to Contact**: `contact.account_ref` references `account.id`.
- **Account to Project**: `project.client_id` references `account.id`.
- **Opportunity to Project**: `project.opportunity_ref` references `opportunity.id`.
- **Account to Asset**: `asset.client` references `account.id` or `account.name`.
- **Project to Asset**: `asset.project` references `project.id`.

### Mappings:
- **Account Tier**: `Bronze`, `Gold`, `Silver`, `Platinum` in the source map directly to the enum in the target.
- **Opportunity Stage**: `Prospecting`, `Qualification`, `Closed Lost`, `Closed Won` in the source map directly to the enum in the target.
- **Project Status**: `Completed`, `On Hold`, `In Planning`, `Cancelled` in the source map to `Active`, `Completed`, `In Planning`, `On Hold`, `Cancelled` in the target.
- **Contact Role**: Not present in the source, so we need to determine a default or mapping.
- **Contact Preferred Language**: Not present in the source, so we need to determine a default or mapping.

### Missing Data:
- **Account**: `ERP_Number__c`, `Website`, `BillingCity`, `BillingCountry`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source. We need to provide defaults or mappings.
- **Contact**: `Phone`, `Title`, `Role__c`, `Preferred_Language__c`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Opportunity**: `CloseDate`, `CurrencyIsoCode`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Project__c**: `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` are not in the source.

### Defaults:
- For missing `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`, we can use `NULL` or a default timestamp.
- For `IsDeleted`, we can default to `0` (false).
- For `Website`, `BillingCity`, `BillingCountry`, we can use `NULL`.
- For `Role__c` and `Preferred_Language__c`, we can use `NULL` or a default value.
- For `CloseDate`, we can use `NULL` or a default date.
- For `CurrencyIsoCode`, we can default to `EUR` or `NULL`.

### Key Mappings:
- **Account to Opportunity**: Since `opportunity.customer_number` is not in the `account` table, but `opportunity.account_name` matches `account.name`, we can join `opportunity` to `account` using `account_name = account.name`.
- **Asset to Account**: `asset.client` can be either an `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`) or an `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`). We need to handle both cases.

### Next Steps:
1. **Account Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `tier` to `Customer_Tier__c`, `region` to `Region__c`, `industry` to `Industry`. Use `NULL` for missing fields.
2. **Contact Model**: Split `full_name` into `FirstName` and `LastName`. Map `account_ref` to `AccountId` by joining to `account.id`.
3. **Opportunity Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `stage` to `StageName`, `amount` to `Amount`. Join to `account` using `account_name = account.name` to get `AccountId`.
4. **Project__c Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `status` to `Project_Status__c`, `go_live` to `Go_Live_Date__c`. Join to `account` using `client_id = account.id` to get `Account__c`. Join to `opportunity` using `opportunity_ref = opportunity.id` to get `Opportunity__c`.
5. **Installed_Asset__c Model**: Map `id` to `Id`, `name` to `Name`, `serial` to `Serial_Number__c`, `warranty` to `Warranty_End_Date__c`. Handle `client` to `Account__c` by joining to `account` using either `client = account.id` or `client = account.name`. Join to `project` using `project = project.id` to get `Project__c`.

### Handling `asset.client`:
The `asset.client` field can be either:
- An `account.id` (e.g., `ACC-1005`)
- An `account.name` (e.g., `Gnatz AG`)

We need to join `asset` to `account` using a `COALESCE` or `CASE` to handle both cases.

### Handling `full_name` Splitting:
We can split `full_name` into `FirstName` and `LastName` using `SPLIT_PART` or `REGEXP_REPLACE`.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="The `opportunity` table shows that: - Each `account_name` is associated with exactly one `customer_number`. - There are some inconsistencies in `account_name` (e.g., `Lindau Neuschäfer GRb` vs `Lindau Neuschäfer GbR`). ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='version: 2

sources:
  - name: fixture_missing_relations_v2_src
    schema: "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"
    tables:
      - name: account
      - name: contact
      - name: opportunity

', tool_call_id='QEeTLJ3Hq', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 7, 48, 974748, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    a.id AS "Id",
    a.name AS "Name",
    NULL AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN a.tier IS NOT NULL THEN INITCAP(a.tier)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    a.region AS "Region__c",
    a.industry AS "Industry",
    NULL AS "Website",
    NULL AS "BillingCity",
    NULL AS "BillingCountry",
    o.customer_number AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} a
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT DISTINCT account_name, customer_number
    FROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
) o ON a.name = o.account_name
', tool_call_id='uorB333m1', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 7, 48, 975182, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    c.id AS "Id",
    CASE
        WHEN c.full_name IS NOT NULL THEN TRIM(SPLIT_PART(c.full_name, ' ', 1))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "FirstName",
    CASE
        WHEN c.full_name IS NOT NULL AND c.full_name ~ ' ' THEN TRIM(SUBSTRING(c.full_name FROM POSITION(' ' IN c.full_name) + 1))
        ELSE ''
    END AS "LastName",
    c.email AS "Email",
    NULL AS "Phone",
    NULL AS "Title",
    NULL AS "Role__c",
    NULL AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    a.id AS "AccountId",
    c.id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'contact') }} c
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} a
    ON c.account_ref = a.id
', tool_call_id='V1BMnq4fV', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 7, 48, 975585, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",
    o.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN o.stage IS NOT NULL THEN INITCAP(o.stage)
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    '1970-01-01' AS "CloseDate",
    o.amount AS "Amount",
    'EUR' AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    a.id AS "AccountId",
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_missing_relations_v2_src', 'account') }} a
    ON TRIM(LOWER(o.account_name)) = TRIM(LOWER(a.name))
', tool_call_id='jiEurEJyQ', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 7, 48, 976003, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    p.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN p.status = 'Completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN p.status = 'On Hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN p.status = 'In Planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN p.status = 'Cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        WHEN p.status = 'Active' THEN 'Active'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live

    a.id AS "Account__c",

', tool_call_id='r6R8pEW5S', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 14, 7, 48, 976395, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    a.id AS "Id",

    CASE

    COALESCE(
